title: Cloudflare Tunnel Health
spl:
index=zt sourcetype="cloudflare:tunnel" earliest=-24h
| eval healthy=if(match(lower(event_type),"(?i)connected|healthy|active"),1,0)
| stats latest(healthy) as status latest(_time) as last_seen by tunnel_id, tunnel_name, connector_id
| where status=0 OR last_seen < relative_time(now(), "-1h")
| sort last_seen
description: Cloudflare Tunnels (formerly Argo Tunnels) connect private infrastructure to Cloudflare without opening inbound ports. Tunnel health determines whether users can reach private applications through Cloudflare Access.
value: Cloudflare Tunnels (formerly Argo Tunnels) connect private infrastructure to Cloudflare without opening inbound ports. Tunnel health determines whether users can reach private applications through Cloudflare Access.
implementation: Configure Logpush for Tunnel health events. Alert when tunnels disconnect for >10 minutes. Track connector version compliance. Report on tunnel availability SLA.
visualization: Single value (tunnels down), Table (unhealthy tunnels), Line chart (tunnel availability %), Timeline (connect/disconnect events).
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Cloudflare App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4501).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: Cloudflare Logpush (Tunnel logs), `sourcetype=cloudflare:tunnel`.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Configure Logpush for Tunnel health events. Alert when tunnels disconnect for >10 minutes. Track connector version compliance. Report on tunnel availability SLA.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=zt sourcetype="cloudflare:tunnel" earliest=-24h
| eval healthy=if(match(lower(event_type),"(?i)connected|healthy|active"),1,0)
| stats latest(healthy) as status latest(_time) as last_seen by tunnel_id, tunnel_name, connector_id
| where status=0 OR last_seen < relative_time(now(), "-1h")
| sort last_seen
```

Understanding this SPL

**Cloudflare Tunnel Health** — Cloudflare Tunnels (formerly Argo Tunnels) connect private infrastructure to Cloudflare without opening inbound ports. Tunnel health determines whether users can reach private applications through Cloudflare Access.

Documented **Data sources**: Cloudflare Logpush (Tunnel logs), `sourcetype=cloudflare:tunnel`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Cloudflare App for Splunk (Splunkbase 4501). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: zt; **sourcetype**: cloudflare:tunnel. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=zt, sourcetype="cloudflare:tunnel", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **healthy** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by tunnel_id, tunnel_name, connector_id** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where status=0 OR last_seen < relative_time(now(), "-1h")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the Cloudflare dashboard for the same zones, rules, and time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Single value (tunnels down), Table (unhealthy tunnels), Line chart (tunnel availability %), Timeline (connect/disconnect events).
grandmaExplanation: We use network security tools to watch who accesses what, help prevent data leaks, enforce network isolation, and secure cloud and remote access.
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src span=1h
| sort - count
